coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical analysis of a hybrid cooling concept for an electric battery module
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Fluid Flow
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahid, Seham
Agelin-Chaab, Martin
coredata.dc:description: This paper introduces a novel hybrid thermal management concept, which use secondary coolants (air and water) to extract heat from a phase change material (paraffin), resulting in increased heat extraction capability of the paraffin and improvement the overall thermal performance of the battery module. This concept was analyzed using experimental and numerical studies. The experimental studies were conducted to obtain the temperature and heat flux profiles of the battery module. The numerical model is developed and validated using the experimental data obtained. The outcome of the study showed that the battery module temperature reached the phase change temperature threshold of the PCM in the fifth discharge cycle, and the through the use of latent heat the battery module temperature was maintained between 42 and 44 °C. Additionally, the thermal uniformity started to increase once the PCM started to change and the PCM utilization was ∼2.3 % after seven discharge cycles. Therefore, the proposed thermal management concept for the battery module can sustain the thermal environment needed for effective operation of the Lithium-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change material
Li-ion cylindrical cells
Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) analysis
Battery thermal management
Hybrid cooling
Liquid channels

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: International Journal of Heat and Fluid Flow

Heat flux profile from Experiment I
Temperature profile of battery module from Experiment II
Thermal analysis of single discharge cycle
Thermal analysis of multiple discharge cycles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Top view, (b) A-A section view, (c) B-B section view, and (d) isometric view of the proposed concept CAD model.
Experiment I - (a) setup and (b) experimental schematics.
Experiment II - (a) setup and (b) experimental schematics.
(a) Velocity profiles and (b) average velocity based on number of elements.
Velocity profiles for the different time step sizes.
Transient heat flux at 1C discharge rate.
Comparison of numerical and experimental results for model validation.
(a) Temperature profiles and (b) temperature uniformity profile of the battery module.
Temperature contours at the end of the discharge cycle for (a) cell and (b) PCM at the mid-plane of the battery module.
Maximum and minimum temperature profiles for (a) Discharge Cycle 2, (b) Discharge Cycle 3, and (c) Discharge Cycle 4.
Maximum and minimum temperature profiles for (a) Discharge Cycle 5, (b) Discharge Cycle 6, and (c) Discharge Cycle 7.
Temperature contours of cells at the end of (a) Discharge Cycle 2, (b) Discharge Cycle 3, (c) Discharge Cycle 4, (d) Discharge Cycle 5, (e) Discharge Cycle 6, and (f) Discharge Cycle 7.

Temperature contours of PCM at the mid-plane at the end of (a) Discharge Cycle 2, (b) Discharge Cycle 3, (c) Discharge Cycle 4, (d) Discharge Cycle 5, (e) Discharge Cycle 6, and (f) Discharge Cycle 7.
Samsung INR 18650-25R cell specifications (Samsung SDI Co, 2014).
Physical properties of materials used for Experiment II.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The experiment was repeated three times for 1C discharge rate, and the uncertainty analysis was conducted using the method suggested by Moffat (Moffat, 1988) to estimate the accuracy of the experimental results. Based on the uncertainty analysis, the average percentage uncertainty in the temperature measurement was estimated to be ±3.59 %.

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical analysis of a hybrid cooling concept for an electric battery module

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Ontario Tech University, 2000 Simcoe Street North, Oshawa, Ontario L1H 7K4, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Ontario Tech University, 2000 Simcoe Street North, Oshawa, Ontario L1H 7K4, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper introduces a novel hybrid thermal management concept, which use secondary coolants (air and water) to extract heat from a phase change material (paraffin), resulting in increased heat extraction capability of the paraffin and improvement the overall thermal performance of the battery module. This concept was analyzed using experimental and numerical studies. The experimental studies were conducted to obtain the temperature and heat flux profiles of the battery module. The numerical model is developed and validated using the experimental data obtained. The outcome of the study showed that the battery module temperature reached the phase change temperature threshold of the PCM in the fifth discharge cycle, and the through the use of latent heat the battery module temperature was maintained between 42 and 44 °C. Additionally, the thermal uniformity started to increase once the PCM started to change and the PCM utilization was ∼2.3 % after seven discharge cycles. Therefore, the proposed thermal management concept for the battery module can sustain the thermal environment needed for effective operation of the Lithium-ion batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: user defined function for continuity equation

ce:section.ce:para: A major issue encountered by the transportation industry is the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions as they are responsible for 29 % of the overall emissions (Fast Facts on Transportation Greenhouse Gas Emissions | US EPA n.d.) and to overcome this issue transportation electrification was introduced. Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are currently the leading batteries for developing electric vehicle (EV) packs. This is mainly due to the low rate of self-discharge of Li-ion cells, high energy density, and extended lifecycle (Omar et al., 2014; Sefidan et al., 2017; Saw et al., 2018). However, thermal issues related to Li-ion batteries needs resolution as they require a particular thermal atmosphere for effective performance (Rao et al., 2011).

The thermal management strategies (TMS) are based on the primary cooling strategies that include air, liquid, and phase change material (PCM). Air cooling based TMS is the most cost-effective system, however air has limited cooling capabilities due to its low thermal properties (Wei and Agelin-Chaab, 2019). Liquids generally have higher cooling capacities compared to air, therefore they are capable of improved cooling and can effectively maintain the required thermal environment for operation of Li-ion cells (Shahid et al., 2022). The simplest form of liquid based TMS is designed using electrically insulating fluids. These fluids extract heat from the cell’s surface through direct contact (Park and Jung, 2013; Patil et al., 2020; Dubey et al., 2021). Usually there is an increased viscosity associated with electrically insulating fluids which results in the requirement of high pumping power. Moreover, in large battery packs, there is an increased risk of leakage of the fluid that can cause unfavorable effects on the electrical battery pack (Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2022). To counter these issues, electrically non-insulating fluids are utilized. They have lower viscosities compared to insulating fluids, therefore they require less pumping power. In order to incorporate them into the battery pack, cold plates are used that contain these fluids. The cold plates are connected to the cells’ surface to extract heat. However, the application of the cold plates is restricted to rectangular cell geometries because the degree of manufacturing difficulty for cylindrical cells is higher (Panchal et al., 2017; Panchal et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). An alternative to a cold plate is to use serpentine liquid channels are developed that run through the cylindrical cell rows. These liquid channels contain electrically non-insulating fluids. In these cases, the part of the cell in contact with the channels cools at a higher rate than the part that is not in contact, causing temperature non-uniformity to occur. Zhao et al. (Zhao et al., 2019) developed multiple configurations of serpentine channels, and temperature uniformity occurred through increment in surface area that is in contact with the liquid channels in the streamwise direction. Furthermore, to eliminate the restrictions of using cold plates for cylindrical cells, Rao et al. (Rao et al., 2017) used variable-sized blocks of aluminum placed in contact with the cylindrical cells and the length of the block was varied to achieve uniform cooling through the battery pack. Liquid channels ran within these aluminum blocks to extract heat from the blocks. Furthermore, Subhedar et al. (Subhedar et al., 2023) studied the flow of nano-fluids through the battery pack housing cylindrical Li-ion cells. The results showed that with a volume fraction of 4 % of Al2O3/EG-Water nanofluid the temperature of the battery module was maintained below 50 °C. Additionally, in order to reduce the energy requirement of the TMS, Xie et al. (Xie et al., 2022) developed a novel liquid cooling plate which accounted for only a 16.4 wt% module weight and maintained the maximum temperature below 31.8 °C and the temperature uniformity below 3.7 °C. Liquid cooling is effective at extracting heat from the cells, however high pumping power causes a hindrance in its application within the battery packs (He et al., 2017).

PCMs are also effective in extracting heat from the cells using latent heat. Initially, its use was suggested by Al-Hallaj and Selman (Al-Hallaj and Selman, 2000) and later on Javani et al. (Javani et al., 2015) showed that a high a thermal uniformity is achievable in battery packs. Jiang et al. (Jiang et al., 2017) applied a composite PCM (CPCM) and maximum temperatures were limited to 41–44 °C at 5C cell discharge rate. The C-rate is defined as the charge/discharge current divided by nominally rated battery capacity. This CPCM was developed by using a paraffin base and combining it with expanded graphite (EG) to increase thermal conductivity. In comparison, through forced air cooling of 1 m/s, 72 °C maximum temperature was observed. He et al. (He et al., 2018) conducted an investigation with the EG/paraffin CPCM and results concluded 2.82 °C of temperature uniformity with 7 % of EG. This was considered as the optimum EG proportion within CPCM. Furthermore, a novel PCM composition was developed and investigated by Hussain et al. (Hussain et al., 2018). For the composite PCM the nickel foam was coated with saturated paraffin and graphene. It was concluded that the thermal conductivity of CPCM increased 23 folds when compared to pure paraffin. A novel CPCM consisting of dual regions of two different phase change temperatures was designed by Ye et al. (Ye et al., 2022). The lower phase change temperature region was activated at regular ambient temperatures. This enabled the TMS to maintain the battery module temperature between 25.9–34.9 °C. Similarly, at high ambient temperatures the higher phase change temperature region was activated. Through this the battery module temperature was kept below 49.2 °C. In addition to dual phase change temperature regions, EG was added to CPCM and the thermal conductivity of the CPCM was increased to 1.98 W/m.K. Lv et al. (Lv et al., 2020) developed a novel PCM serpentine channel which increased the heat transfer surface area and increased the energy density of the battery module by 13.8 Wh/kg. The results showed that when compared to the block shaped PCM the serpentine shaped PCM reduces the maximum temperature of the battery module by 2.3 °C under same airflow power. Furthermore Lv et al. (Lv et al., 2020) studied the durability of the PCM through 200 cycles and the improvement in the battery deterioration. The results showed that in addition to improving the cooling of the battery module the lifecycle of the battery module with PCM could be improved by 65.3 %. Additionally, a flexible CPCM was developed by Wu et al. (Wu et al., 2022). The flexibility of the CPCM allowed it to encase the cylindrical geometry of the cell completely which increased the contact surface area and reduced the thermal contact resistance. The novel CPCM had a thermal conductivity of 1.64 W/m.K and it exhibited a better flexibility between −5 to 20 °C. When compared to a rigid CPCM the results concluded a 4 °C reduction in the maximum temperature. Joshi et al. (Joshi et al., 2023) studied the pre-heating of batteries using fins and PCM. A numerical analysis was carried out and the varying effects of PCM thickness was examined. The study showed promising results by combining fins and PCM on the thermal management of batteries. Additionally, Bais et al. (Bais et al., 2023) conducted an experimentally study by wrapping RT-47 paraffin PCM in a 4 mm cavity around the cylindrical cell. The results shows that the temperature of the cell increased at an average rate of 0.0219 °C/min which resulted in ∼73 % improvement compared to bare cells.

The PCM application in TMS exhibits promising results, however its low overall heat transfer coefficient results in a hindrance in its mass adoption. When the PCM is utilized and completely melted then a secondary TMS is needed to charge the PCM.

Since there are a few drawbacks of using the cooling mediums individually in a TMS, therefore hybrid TMS are introduced that combine two or more cooling mediums. A simple hybrid system is to combine air and liquid cooling by incorporating mist into the airflow. This was studied by Saw et al. (Saw et al., 2018) and it was found that 3 % of mist within the airflow of 5 g/s is required to keep the cell temperatures below 40 °C. Moreover, the natural evaporation of water was utilized by Wei and Agelin-Chaab (Wei and Agelin-Chaab, 2019; Wei and Agelin-Chaab, 2018). Water channel strips within the battery pack were exposed to airflow. Through this, the water vapors were formed using natural evaporation and were incorporated in the airflow stream. Additionally, the air particles regained their heat capacity once they interacted with the water strips. This resulted in an improvement of 56 % in the uniformity and 20 % in temperature reduction. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2017) studied a hybrid TMS by combining cold plates with airflow. The cold plates were stationed below the cells, and the airflow circulated through cold plate surfaces to extract heat from the cold plates. Multiple strategies and concepts were tested. The temperature uniformity increased by 2.42 times, and a further 3.45 °C maximum temperature reduction was achieved. In the authors’ previous study (Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2021) a novel design was developed in which liquid jackets were attached to each cylindrical cell. The liquid jackets contained fluid, and since it was isolated to each cell, therefore the design was capable of employing direct cooling by using electrically non-insulating fluids. Moreover, passive turbulence generating techniques developed in earlier studies (Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2018; Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2017; Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2018; Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2019) were incorporated to improve mixing of the air stream which resulted in a 1.26 °C of temperature uniformity.

This paper introduces a novel hybrid thermal management concept for Li-ion battery modules. It consists of primary cooling through PCM and secondary cooling through vertical liquid channels between the PCM and airflow at the top of the cells. The fluid within the liquid channel is stationary instead of continuous flowing fluid in liquid-based TMSs in the open literature. Moreover, the natural evaporation of fluid is utilized to remove heat from the PCM material, thereby increasing the cooling capacity of the PCM. The proposed concept can be scaled up or down based on the energy requirements of the vehicle. The primary objective is to keep the temperature of the cells in the optimum range of 25–40 °C, achieve a high temperature uniformity, and utilize the least amount of PCM. In addition, a secondary objective is to significantly reduce the pumping power usually required for pumping fluids and to reduce the airflow velocity, which reduces the fan power by considering a laminar flow. Finally, environmentally friendly fluid such as water is used to improve sustainability and eliminate the emission of harmful gases and vapors into the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the proposed hybrid TMS
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed hybrid TMS concept is based on a single primary and two secondary coolants. The schematic of the concept is shown in Fig. 1 . It consists of cylindrical Li-ion cells which are in direct contact with the surrounding PCM material. The liquid channels are placed vertically in between the PCM, and stationary fluid is present within these channels. An air duct is stationed on the upper surface of the of the battery module, and the opening of liquid channel is in the air duct.

There are three stages of heat removal in the proposed concept. Initially the heat generated from the cell is absorbed by the PCM material using latent heat. Then the heat from PCM is extracted through liquid cooling. Finally, the fluid evaporates through natural evaporation, and the vapors enter the air duct. The airflow stream in the air duct then removes the evaporated fluid from the battery module into the environment. The fluid in the liquid channels is topped up using capillary action through hydrophilic material placed within these channels. The computer aided design (CAD) model of the proposed hybrid TMS concept is shown in Fig. 2 .

In this study, two separate experiments were conducted. In the Experiment I, a single Samsung INR18650-25R cell was used to obtain the heat flux profiles at the cell’s surface. The heat flux profile was obtained to be used as a boundary condition in the numerical modeling. In Experiment II, the battery module was manufactured, as shown in Fig. 2, with nine Samsung INR18650-25R cells and the temperature measurements were obtained. The properties of the cell are provided in Table 1 . The detailed experimental setup and procedure are described in the below subsections.

Experiment I was conducted to acquire the heat flux measurements at the cell’s surface. This will then be used in numerical modeling as the transient boundary. The experimental setup is shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the schematics are shown in Fig. 3(b). Samsung INR18650-25R cell with Aluminum tabs attached to positive and negative terminals was used for this experiment. The electrical insulation at the surface of the cells was detached to get accurate measurements, as the non-insulated cells are used in the battery module. For charging of the cells Turnigy Reaktor Quadkore charger was used. It has the capability to charge 4 cell groups simultaneously up to 300 W and 20A. The charger was powered through a power supply (Jesverty SPS-3010 N). To discharge the cells a load discharger (TDI RBL488) was used. It has high power draining load capacities of up to 800 W and 120A. It incorporates variable speed forced air cooling to ensure a quiet environment. To measure the heat flux profiles Omega HFS-5 was. In order to measure the readings from the HFS-5 sensor, a National Instrument (NI) data acquisition device (DAQ) NI-9211 combined with NI cDAQ-9174 chassis was used and LabView software obtained the DAQ module readings.

As shown in Fig. 3(a), alligator clips were connected to the electrode tabs of the Li-ion cell. The cell was charged in two stages. The first stage of the charging process comprised of constant current (CC) charge at 1 A. During this stage, the voltage of the cell increased gradually. Once the voltage reached 4.2 V then it was charged at constant voltage (CV) of 4.2 V, and the charging current was reduced from 1 A to 0 A. Once the cell was completely charged, then it was kept at an ambient environment of 25 °C for 1 h. The discharge cycle was then initiated, and the cell was discharged at 2.5 A CC discharge (which corresponds to 1C) until the voltage reached 2.5 V cut-off voltage. During the discharge process, the data was logged using the LabView.

The experiment was repeated three times for 1C discharge rate, and the error analysis was conducted using the technique recommended by Moffat (Moffat, 1988). Based on the uncertainty analysis, the average percentage uncertainty in the heat flux measurement was estimated to be ±3.59 %.

Experiment II was conducted to measure the battery module’s temperature profiles. The setup is shown in Fig. 4 (a), and the schematics are shown in Fig. 4(b). The specifics of the charger and discharger, power supply, NI DAQ module, and LabView software are already provided in the previous section. For this experiment, the battery module was manufactured as per the design provided in Fig. 2. The outer casing of the module was made from wood. The cells were placed within the battery module with a series connection. Aluminum liquid channels were filled with water and placed in between the PCM material. An axial fan was used to force air through the air duct in the battery module, and the velocity of the airflow was measured using the Proster Anemometer. Finally, the temperature was measured using nine T-type thermocouples attached to each cell.

To manufacture the battery module, the panels of the wooden housing were laser cut, including the holes in the bottom and the top plates to hold the cells and liquid channels in place. The wooden panels were then glued together. All nine cells were then placed inside the housing. Four aluminum liquid channels were placed inside the housing and filled with water. Thermocouples were then attached to each cell using adhesive tapes. Then the PCM blocks (Paraffin wax) were melted using a wax melt warmer, and the melted PCM was then poured into the battery pack. The melted PCM was poured to ensure complete surface area of the cell was in direct contact with PCM once it solidified. The wires were then connected to the tabs of the cells to connect them all in series. Finally, the air duct was placed on the module.

The procedure for Experiment II consisted of charging the cells using the Turnigy Reaktor charger. The cells were charged with CC-CV process. Since all the cells are connected in series, therefore the total battery module voltage at the end of the charging cycle was 37.8 V (the voltage of each cell was 4.2 V). The battery module was maintained in an ambient temperature of 25 °C. The cells were discharged at CC once the measurements from the thermocouples reached 25 °C. The readings were logged in using LabView. Once the voltage of the battery module reached the cut-off voltage of 22.5 V, the discharge process was terminated. Details of the various materials used for Experiment II is provided in Table 2 .

The numerical model was developed for the battery module described in Section 3.2 (Experiment II) using ANSYS Fluent. The solidification/melting, energy, and flow models in Fluent were coupled to achieve the numerical model for this concept. The airflow was considered as a laminar flow, therefore turbulence modeling was not applied. The governing equations for the energy and fluid flow models are as follows (Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2023).

Continuity Equation

In the PCM region the solidification/melting model was applied as phase change will occur in the PCM using latent heat. The equations related to this model are provided below (Shahid and Agelin-Chaab, 2023).

The cross section of the developed grid is shown in Fig. 5 . Since the air flows within the duct region only, therefore inflation layers were added to the walls in this region to capture the boundary layer.

In order to verify that the results are not dependent on the size of elements in the grid, a grid independence study was performed. A coarse mesh, medium mesh, medium-fine mesh, and fine mesh were developed. In each subsequent mesh, the total elements were increased by approximately 50 %. To compare the results, airflow velocity profiles were obtained and compared, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). In addition, the average velocity is shown in Fig. 6(b). Based on the results, it can be seen that there is no significant difference beyond the grid size of 614,024 elements; therefore the medium mesh was selected.

Moreover, a time independence study (TIS) was conducted to make sure that the results are not dependent on the time step. An optimum time step size is required, as a large time step will produce inaccurate results, and a small time step will increase the computational time significantly. The results are provided in Fig. 7 for the TIS. Based on the results, the time step size of 0.5 s was selected.

For this study transient simulations were conducted. At the surface of the cells, the transient boundary condition of heat flux at 1C was employed. This heat flux profile was obtained from Experiment I. The ambient condition was set to 25 °C at atmospheric pressure. The inlet airflow velocity was set to 1.6 m/s based on the velocity obtained from the anemometer in Experiment II (Section 3.2). Finally, the material properties were applied to respective materials, as shown in Table 2. Furthermore, six additional discharge cycles were run for a total of 7 discharge cycles. The results and conditions at the end of the preceding discharge cycle were used as the initial starting point for the start of the subsequent discharge cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat flux profile from Experiment I
ce:section.ce:para: The heat flux profile at 1C charge rate is shown in Fig. 8 . At 1C discharge rate, a 2,500 mAh cell discharges at 2.5 A for 3,600 s (or 1 h). Therefore, the discharge cycle in Fig. 8 ends at 3,600 s. It can be seen that the heat flux initially increases gradually up to ∼83 W/m2 until ∼1,240 s, and then it decreases slightly to ∼70 W/m2 at 2,000 s. It increases again, and then the curve plateaus at approximately 3,000 s. In the final discharge stages, from 3,400 to 3,600 s, the heat flux increases sharply to the highest point of 170 W/m2. This heat flux profile was used as the boundary condition at the surface of the cells for the numerical modeling, as stated in Section 4.2. A sixth order polynomial trendline was fitted to the transient results, as shown by the dotted line in Fig. 8. The R2 – value of the fitted trendline was 0.9889, which confirms that the trendline follows closely to the actual heat flux measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature profile of battery module from Experiment II
ce:section.ce:para: Experiment II was conducted at a 1C discharge rate. Since the cells were connected in series, therefore the cells were discharged at 2.5 A at an ambient of 25 °C. The transient temperature profile is shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that the temperature increases in all the cells follow the same trend. This trend corresponds to the heat flux profile shown in Fig. 8. In areas where the heat flux reduces, the temperature increase rate also reduces and where the heat flux increases, the temperature increase rate also increases. At the end of the discharge cycle, the maximum temperature of the battery module was ∼32.5 °C, and the minimum temperature was ∼31.3 °C. This corresponds to a temperature uniformity of 1.2 °C.

The highest temperatures are exhibited by cell numbers C5, C6 and C8. Cell C5 is in middle of the battery module, therefore the heat from the surrounding cells impacts the cell in the middle resulting in the highest module temperatures. Moreover, for cells C6 and C8 the thermocouple was placed facing the battery module center, therefore the surface of the cell that is facing the battery module center has a higher temperature. In contrast, cells C1 and C2 show the lowest temperatures, and this is because the thermocouple in these cells is placed on the surface of the cell facing the housing of the battery module. Additionally, cells C4 and C7 show intermediate temperature values of the battery module. In the case of cells C4 and C7 the thermocouple was placed on the surface facing each other. This is how there is temperature non-uniformity in the cells. The cell’s surface facing the center of the battery module has the highest temperature, whereas the surface of the cell that is facing the neighboring cells have intermediate temperatures, and the surface of the cell facing the housing of the battery module has the lowest temperatures. Also, the temperature difference between the different cells shows the temperature non-uniformity within the battery module as well. As stated earlier, the primary objective of developing this concept is to maintain the temperature uniformity to within 5 °C. However, due to the limited measuring capabilities of the experimental setup, it was not possible to measure the temperature of each cell at different surface points. Therefore, a numerical model was developed, and numerical simulations were performed to obtain the surface contours of each cell in the module.

The numerical simulations provide effective results, however it is important to validate the results of the numerical model with the experimental results. Since the numerical model is based on the battery module developed for Experiment II, therefore the results of Experiment II were used for validation. The validation results are shown in Fig. 10 . It can be seen that the numerical results are within the ± % temperature error bars which falls within the ±3.59 % experimental uncertainty. Therefore, the numerical model accurately predicts the temperature profile of the battery module is considered as completely validated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal analysis of single discharge cycle
ce:section.ce:para: Using the developed and validated numerical model, simulations were conducted using the methodology described in Section 4. In order for the Li-ion cells to perform effectively, a specific thermal environment is required. The temperatures of the cells can range from 25 to 45 °C. Moreover, maintaining temperature uniformity within each cell and within the entire battery module is vital. A high temperature uniformity occurs when the temperature difference is low, and a low temperature uniformity occurs when the temperature differences are high. An overview of the maximum and minimum temperature exhibited by the battery module and its temperature uniformity is shown in Fig. 11 .

The maximum temperature at the end of the discharge cycles reached 31.5 °C, and the minimum temperature reached 27.4 °C. According to Fig. 11(a), the increase in maximum temperature is at an elevated rate compared to the rate of minimum temperature increase. Additionally, the temperature uniformity also reduces due to the different temperature increase rates. At the termination of the discharge cycle the temperature uniformity is still high, with a value of 2.1 °C.

For an in-depth understanding, the temperature contours of the cells once the discharge cycle terminates are shown in Fig. 12 (a). Moreover, temperature contours for the PCM material at the center plane of the battery module are shown in Fig. 12(b). It can be seen from Fig. 12(a) that the high temperature locations are the middle surfaces of the cells. The surfaces of the cells that are facing the bordering cells have higher temperatures compared to the surfaces facing the battery module housing due to the compound heating effect from the neighboring cells. Additionally, the surfaces that are attached to bottom and top module housings have comparatively lower temperatures. The is mainly because of the conduction of heat that occurs from the cell’s surface to the top and bottom plates. Furthermore, additional heat transfer occurs from the top plate of the housing to the airflow which results in an even lower temperature of the cell’s surface attached to the top plate.

The compound heating that occurs in between the adjacent neighboring cells can be seen in Fig. 12(b). The PCM sandwiched between the surfaces of the two adjacent cells has the highest temperature as there is no additional heat sink directly between the cells for the heat to dissipate. For all the other surfaces there are additional heat sinks in the form of module housing and liquid channels. Therefore, the temperature is lower in these areas. Additionally, the phase change does not occur and latent heat of the PCM is not utilized as the temperature of the cells do not reach the PCM’s phase change temperature. It is only using the thermal conductivity and heat capacity to transfer heat through conduction during this discharge cycle. Therefore, to assess the thermal performance of the proposed concept multiple discharge cycles were simulated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal analysis of multiple discharge cycles
ce:section.ce:para: Six more discharge cycles were simulated in addition to the first one described in Section 5.4. The final conditions at the end of the first cycle were used as the initial condition for the second discharge cycle. This was then repeated for all the subsequent discharge cycles. The temperature profiles for the maximum and minimum temperatures for discharge cycles 2, 3, and 4 are shown in Fig. 13 . It can be seen that the maximum temperature increases up to 35.3 °C in discharge cycle 2, and then it increases to 39 °C in discharge cycle 3, and finally to 41.7 °C in discharge cycle 4. Initially, the heat removal from the PCM is low at lower temperatures, and the rate of temperature increase is low, however as the temperatures approach the PCM’s melting temperature, the heat removal of the PCM increases, resulting in a lower temperature increase rate.

The maximum temperature reached the PCM’s melting temperature in the fourth discharge cycle. Therefore, three additional discharge cycles were simulated, and the temperature profiles are provided in Fig. 14 . The melting temperature of the PCM ranges between 42 and 44 °C as stated in Table 2. Hence, it can be seen that the maximum temperature in discharge cycles 5, 6, and 7 stays within the phase change temperature range. From discharge cycle 5 the PCM starts to melt, and any further heat generated in the cell is removed by the PCM using latent heat.

The temperature contours are provided in Fig. 15 for the battery module at the end of each subsequent discharge cycle. The cooling phenomenon is the same as discussed in the previous section, with the cell’s surface in contact with the top plate exhibiting lower temperatures due to air cooling through the air duct. The difference starting from discharge cycle 3 and through all the subsequent cycles can be seen in the cell surfaces that are facing the center cell in the module. The temperature on the surface towards the center of the battery module is lower as compared to surfaces facing the module housing. This is due to the additional cooling effect that is achieved through liquid cooling. Since some of the heat from the PCM is extracted using the water in the liquid channels, which is then expelled into the airflow, the PCM regains its heat extracting capability and extracts more heat from these surfaces. Whereas there is no additional cooling in the surface area that is exposed to the module housing. This can also be seen in Fig. 16 in which the contours of the PCM temperature are shown at the end of each subsequent discharge cycles. The PCM temperature in between the adjacent neighboring cells and the module housing keeps on increasing in each subsequent discharge cycle, whereas the temperature in the center around the liquid cooling channels is relatively low.

It can also be seen from Fig. 16 that the temperature of water in the first column of liquid channels is low when compared to the second column of liquid channels. This is mainly due to the laminar airflow. The airflow extracts heat from the first columns, and then once it reaches the second columns, it has less capacity for heat extraction resulting in a relatively higher temperature of water in the second columns. Since there is a lower temperature of the water, it extracts more heat from the surrounding PCM, so the temperature of the PCM is lower in this area and higher in the area of the second column of liquid channels.

The PCM in the proposed concept is confined in the battery module housing and is stationary. Hence, it is vital to extract heat from PCM as well because once the phase change is completed then the temperature of the cells will keep on increasing, which can cause safety issues. As discussed previously, the secondary coolants, have been able to extract heat and increase the overall heat transfer coefficient of the PCM. However, it is crucial to understand the amount of PCM that is changing phase during the discharge cycles. Since the temperature of the PCM reaches its phase change temperature in the fifth discharge, therefore the amount of PCM that changes phase in discharge cycles 5, 6, and 7 are shown in Fig. 17 . In discharge cycle 5 the phase change starts to occur at approximately 1,800 s, and the change occurred in only 0.3 % of the PCM. In discharge cycle 6, melting of the PCM continues as the cell’s heat is transferred to the PCM, and during this cycle additional 1 % of PCM changes its phase. Similarly, in cycle 7, 1 % of PCM changes its phase. This shows that with the secondary coolants included in the hybrid TMS, the amount of PCM that melts are low, and it is capable of extracting additional heat energy from the cells.

In this paper, thermal analysis is conducted for a hybrid thermal management strategy for electrical battery modules. The proposed hybrid concept is based on a single primary and two secondary coolants. Two experimental studies were conducted. In the first experimental study, the heat flux profiles were recorded. In the second study, the battery module was developed, and temperature profiles of all the cells was obtained at 1C discharge rate. A numerical model was also developed based on the proposed concept, and it was validated using the second experimental study. Based on the numerical results, a single discharge cycle was not enough to completely analyze the thermal performance of the proposed concept. Therefore, simulations for six additional discharge cycles were run. Based on the results, the PCM’s melting temperature was reached in the fifth discharge cycle, and the through the use of latent heat, the battery module temperature was kept between 42 and 44 °C. Moreover, the temperature uniformity started to increase once PCM’s melting temperature was reached. Finally, by the end of the last discharge cycle, phase change occurred in only 2.3 % of the PCM. Therefore, it can be concluded that the proposed concept is capable of maintaining the thermal environment required by the Li-ion cells for effective performance.

Seham Shahid: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Martin Agelin-Chaab: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
